filter.div_span_rule: kept ONLY when: has a class AND not decorative AND not a fragment-class AND no control ancestor (button/a/label/summary) AND OWN DIRECT text >= 12 chars. (Own text, not descendant — a wrapper whose words live in children is not a unit; the kept ancestor carries the words.)
filter.note: no dedup — same surface across mockups recurs; RG5 REDUCE collapses

WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the system's live state at a glance (running / thinking / awaiting) and reach every board-level action from one place. ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the canvas — the live board the whole composition sits on (pan, zoom, select; zoom in for more node detail — semantic zoom). ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the palette — the rack of build-blocks (every node-type the system knows, grouped by kind: process · acts, content · holds). ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: a node on the board — the one generic card every node-type renders as (its kind, status, output, address, and its input/output ports). WHAT YOU CAN DO: select it to inspect + edit its config, drag a port-nub to another node to wire it, watch its status colour change as the graph runs. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the card is the single generic NodeShape.tsx (NodeShapeUtil) — it draws every type from the registry, so a new node-type needs zero card code; what a node DOES lives in its nodes/<name>.py.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the canvas — the live board the whole composition sits on (pan, zoom, select; zoom in for more node detail — semantic zoom). ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: a portal — a live window onto another address (one artefact, shown in many places at once, never copied). WHAT YOU CAN DO: see the current value at the address it points to, live — change the source and the portal reflects it, because it is resolved by reference at view-time, not computed. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it's the portal node-type (nodes/portal.py, RESOLVE='reference'); its target is its config.ref — edit that in the inspector to re-point the window.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inspector — the detail panel for the node you have selected (its kind, status, node-id, address, an editable config form, and its output). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read a node's full state, edit its config inline (changes commit immediately), and act on its output (rerun · surface to inbox · build from it · open in workshop). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the panel is Inspector.tsx; the config form is generic from each type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx) — a new node-type gets a form with zero extra code.

candidates.outerHTML: <div class="ws-full">The memo gate is the engine's "re-run only what changed" mechanism. Each node carries a content hash keyed on (type, version, config, input-port hashes). On a run the scheduler compares the freshly-computed key against the stored one: equal → the prior result is served and the node is marked **cached**; different → the node fires, produces a new result, and is marked **ran**. This is why an unchanged branch stays frozen across runs while only the edited path recomputes. It lives in runtime/scheduler.py. Two node kinds are VOLATILE (codebase, model_of_tim) — they bypass the memo gate and always re-read, because their truth is outside the graph (the filesystem, the operato

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: a node on the board — the one generic card every node-type renders as (its kind, status, output, address, and its input/output ports). WHAT YOU CAN DO: select it to inspect + edit its config, drag a port-nub to another node to wire it, watch its status colour change as the graph runs. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the card is the single generic NodeShape.tsx (NodeShapeUtil) — it draws every type from the registry, so a new node-type needs zero card code; what a node DOES lives in its nodes/<name>.py.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the ↻ force / rerun control in the inspector. WHAT YOU CAN DO: force this one node to recompute, bypassing the memo cache (run normally skips a node whose inputs haven't changed; force runs it anyway). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it drives the per-node run path (doRun([nodeId]) → the scheduler); the force-past-memo behaviour lives in runtime/scheduler.py.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the verdict row in a review walk (approve · reject · comment · skip · decide). WHAT YOU CAN DO: give your verdict on the current item — approve it, reject with a reason, comment (capture a note, stays pending), skip (still needs you later), or let the system decide deterministically by consequence; one click = one verdict (the controls lock while a request is in flight). HOW TO CHANGE IT: each verdict goes through suite.py:resolve_surfaced via /api/resolve (operator-only); the build loop reads the verdicts on its next pass.

HOW TO CHANGE IT: each verdict goes through suite.py:resolve_surfaced via /api/resolve (operator-only); the build loop reads the verdicts on its next pass.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the walkthrough card — the visible review organ, shown while a session is active. It runs in two shapes: a REVIEW walk (give a verdict on each pending item) or a guided TOUR (show me how — narrate + spotlight each part of the interface, no verdicts). WHAT YOU CAN DO: step through the session, hear/read each item framed in plain language, and (in a review) record a verdict. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the card is Walkthrough.tsx; the session is suite.py:present_current; verdicts go through /api/resolve.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the right-hand-man — the Company's chat about itself, the one coherent voice that answers from live system state. WHAT YOU CAN DO: ask it about the system (it answers from real state and says so when it can't see something), speak via the mic, configure its model + persona, and approve any action it proposes. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the surface is RhmChat.tsx; the brain is suite.py:chat — your turns are gold (they train the twin), its turns are working-grade inference.

candidates.outerHTML:
How a build comes back

A dispatched build either CLOSES (happy path → the C3 demonstrate-first card) or it returns as one of these — each surfaced loud, never a silent dead end.
failed / refused — needs you
held / deferred — re-offered
design review — FORM half-of-done

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inbox — your chief-of-staff triage deck: the decisions and approvals escalated to you, the decision→build lane, the held/deferred queue, and the resolved-for-you audit. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see what's awaiting you at a glance (by colour and shape, not by reading), drill into any item to decide it, or walk all awaiting items through the review organ. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the deck is Inbox.tsx; the lanes come from suite.py:inbox_lanes (live escalations / resolved / batched), test-pollution filtered at source.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inbox — your chief-of-staff triage deck: the decisions and approvals escalated to you, the decision→build lane, the held/deferred queue, and the resolved-for-you audit. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see what's awaiting you at a glance (by colour and shape, not by reading), drill into any item to decide it, or walk all awaiting items through the review organ. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the deck is Inbox.tsx; the lanes come from suite.py:inbox_lanes (live escalations / resolved / batched), test-pollution filtered at source.

The six returns, by mechanism
verify-failverify-by-use failed → no implemented
scope-overrunchanged > declared; empty = deny-all
FORMsurface touched → design review
launch-failbad claude -p → loud re-queue
cap-deferredover the cap → re-offered, no loss
crashedno terminal event → re-surfaced, no relaunch
Why
candidates.visible_text: The pipeline (CHECK→CLAIM→GATE→LAUNCH→VERIFY→CLOSE) is internal and off both faces. You operate on the , not the circuit — frontstage outcomes, backstage machinery. The six returns, by mechanism verify-by-use failed → no verify-fail implemented changed > declared; empty = deny-all scope-overrun surface touched → design review FORM bad → loud re-queue launch-fail claude -p over the cap → re-offered, no loss cap-deferred no terminal event → re-surfaced, no relaunch crashed Why you only see the return outcome

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inbox — your chief-of-staff triage deck: the decisions and approvals escalated to you, the decision→build lane, the held/deferred queue, and the resolved-for-you audit. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see what's awaiting you at a glance (by colour and shape, not by reading), drill into any item to decide it, or walk all awaiting items through the review organ. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the deck is Inbox.tsx; the lanes come from suite.py:inbox_lanes (live escalations / resolved / batched), test-pollution filtered at source.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the consolidated Settings control-room — the one designed place the system's configuration lives (the brain & persona, presence modes, voice, model roles, and the composition knobs), opened from the ⚙ settings gear. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the live config of every section at a glance and change what's changeable in one place (the model, persona, presence mode, auto-detect, voice ear/engine/path, role bindings); read-only/unavailable settings are shown honestly, never as working. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the surface is Settings.tsx; every write goes through the existing config path (suite.py:set_rhm_config) — it is reuse-only over the real config, not a new store.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inspector — the detail panel for the node you have selected (its kind, status, node-id, address, an editable config form, and its output). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read a node's full state, edit its config inline (changes commit immediately), and act on its output (rerun · surface to inbox · build from it · open in workshop). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the panel is Inspector.tsx; the config form is generic from each type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx) — a new node-type gets a form with zero extra code.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: a config field on the selected node (e.g. the model dropdown). WHAT YOU CAN DO: pick or type a value; a model field offers the REAL models registered at the fabric endpoint (not invented names), and if a saved value isn't in the live list it's shown rather than silently dropped. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the field is generated from the node-type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx); the model list comes from the live registry (suite.py:available_models) — change the field by editing the type's schema.

HOW TO CHANGE IT: the field is generated from the node-type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx); the model list comes from the live registry (suite.py:available_models) — change the field by editing the type's schema.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inspector — the detail panel for the node you have selected (its kind, status, node-id, address, an editable config form, and its output). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read a node's full state, edit its config inline (changes commit immediately), and act on its output (rerun · surface to inbox · build from it · open in workshop). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the panel is Inspector.tsx; the config form is generic from each type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx) — a new node-type gets a form with zero extra code.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the consolidated Settings control-room — the one designed place the system's configuration lives (the brain & persona, presence modes, voice, model roles, and the composition knobs), opened from the ⚙ settings gear. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the live config of every section at a glance and change what's changeable in one place (the model, persona, presence mode, auto-detect, voice ear/engine/path, role bindings); read-only/unavailable settings are shown honestly, never as working. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the surface is Settings.tsx; every write goes through the existing config path (suite.py:set_rhm_config) — it is reuse-only over the real config, not a new store.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the canvas — the live board the whole composition sits on (pan, zoom, select; zoom in for more node detail — semantic zoom). WHAT YOU CAN DO: arrange and connect nodes spatially, select one to inspect it, select two to wire them; the board reflects live run-state. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the board is tldraw driven by NodeShape.tsx (the one generic node shape) + the App.tsx shell; structural canvas changes are external-agent code edits there.

ancestor_dossier.howto: WHAT: the inspector — the detail panel for the node you have selected (its kind, status, node-id, address, an editable config form, and its output). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read a node's full state, edit its config inline (changes commit immediately), and act on its output (rerun · surface to inbox · build from it · open in workshop). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the panel is Inspector.tsx; the config form is generic from each type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx) — a new node-type gets a form with zero extra code.
